sections.title: Core Concepts
items: Single machines hit three physical walls: disk throughput (read speed), memory capacity (RAM), and compute throughput (CPU cores). No software optimization can exceed these physical limits.
The cluster solution divides work across many machines — each with its own disk, memory, and CPU. The pattern is always: divide the data, execute in parallel, combine the results.
Spark keeps intermediate data in memory between steps, eliminating the disk round-trips that made Hadoop MapReduce slow — up to 100x faster for multi-step pipelines.
Every Spark application has one Driver (plans and coordinates work) and one or more Executors (do the actual data processing). Your Python code runs only on the driver.
Data is split into Partitions — smaller chunks distributed across executors. Parallelism = number of partitions processed simultaneously across available cores.
The medallion architecture organises data into Bronze (raw, untouched), Silver (cleaned, typed, validated), and Gold (aggregated, business-ready) layers.
PySpark is a thin Python wrapper. DataFrame operations run inside the JVM on executors — not in Python — making PySpark nearly as fast as Scala Spark.
A Spark job follows four phases: plan building → Catalyst optimisation → stage/task creation → distributed execution. One action triggers one job.

items.label: NordGrid Silver pipeline (read → filter → cast → join → write)
items.code:
meters = spark.read.csv('bronze/meters/2026-03-22.csv', header=True)
meters = meters.filter(col('meter_id').isNotNull())
meters = meters.filter(col('daily_kw').cast('double') > 0)
meters = meters.withColumn('daily_kw', col('daily_kw').cast('double'))
subs   = spark.read.csv('bronze/substations.csv', header=True)
meters = meters.join(subs, 'substation_id', 'left')
meters.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('silver/meters/2026-03-22/')

items.label: Scaling out beats scaling up for large data
items.code:
# 8 x 64 GB machines  =  512 GB total RAM
# 8 x 8 cores         =   64 cores processing in parallel
# 8 x 500 MB/s disks  =  4 GB/s read throughput
# Cost grows linearly; capacity has no hard ceiling

items.label: Job → Stage → Task hierarchy (Spark UI vocabulary)
items.code:
# 1 action  → 1 job
# 1 job     → 1+ stages  (split at shuffle boundaries)
# 1 stage   → 1+ tasks   (1 task per partition)
# 'Stage 2: 200 tasks; slowest task 3 min' = join bottleneck

sections.title: Watch Out
items: Scaling out is not free — a cluster introduces network latency, machine failures, and coordination complexity. Organisations accept this cost because a 35-minute pipeline that must finish in 5 has no alternative.
The bottleneck is not Python being slow. Physical limits (disk speed, RAM size, core count) are the real ceiling — rewriting in a faster language does not change them.
Python UDFs (User-Defined Functions) force data to cross the JVM-Python boundary for every row — dramatically slower than built-in DataFrame operations. Use built-in functions whenever possible.

Local mode does not reflect cluster performance for shuffle-heavy operations (joins, aggregations). A join that takes 2 seconds locally may take 30 seconds on the cluster due to network data movement.

sections.title: Insights
items: Bigger hardware has diminishing returns and hard limits. A single 1 TB RAM server costs ~10x more than ten 128 GB machines combined — and it still cannot process 2 TB in memory.

Catalyst — Spark's query optimiser — rewrites your plan automatically before execution: combining filters, pushing predicates earlier, and broadcasting small tables. Often more effective than manual optimisation.

Bronze is the audit trail. Never transform data in-place at the Bronze layer — if a downstream pipeline produces wrong numbers, raw Bronze data is the only way to diagnose what arrived from the source.
A job's vocabulary (job → stage → task) is what you read in the Spark UI. 'The join stage has 200 tasks and the slowest took 3 minutes' tells an engineer exactly where the bottleneck is.
